Utilities during partial demolition — active constraints and requirements:
- Plan and coordinate: identify affected systems, map shutoffs (main water, main gas, main electrical panel, subpanels), know isolation valves; obtain written contractor schedule for planned shutoffs; provide advance notice to household members.
- Water strategies: isolate work zone and cap branch feeding demo area; use professionally installed temporary water line to a temporary sink if main kitchen sink is offline; consider portable water solutions (bottled water, portable hand-wash station, temporary sink); maintain at least one full bathroom online or arrange porta-potty/temporary hookup if all toilets are offline;

Message:
What are the least invasive accessibility upgrades that provide the biggest quality-of-life gains?

Response:
Good choice — you can get large improvements with minimal disruption. Below are the highest-impact, least-invasive upgrades, with what they accomplish, rough cost cues, and whether they’re DIY or pro-level.

1) Grab bars in key spots  
- Why: dramatically improves safety getting in/out of shower and on/off the toilet.  
- Cost: $30–$150 per bar; professional install $100–$300 each.
